_comment: Cycle-charge-vs-fleeing-target staging: a lone 24-horse cavalry is armed with the Cycle charge stance (J) and sent at a brittle 70-man infantry (morale 2) on a FLAT declared map (terrain deliberately empty so pathfinding cannot shape the arc). The first impact routs the infantry, which flees east; the cavalry retires at a trot, brakes onto the 280 wu standoff, wheels about, canters back in, and lands a second charge on the fleeing target around t950-1000 -- the caracole loop re-engaging a broken foe instead of receding after it forever. all_teams_control keeps team 1's battle AI from re-ordering the infantry before the rout. Sampled ticks pin the story beats (spawn, the approach, the first strike, the rout, the standoff turn, the canter back, the sprint window, the second contact); the post-strike peel-exit windows (roughly t400-500 and past t1000) are deliberately NOT sampled -- while the block reverses out of a landed charge its bodies compress hard (nnd down to ~2.4 wu on 20 wu mounts) and the shape residual spikes to ~80 wu RMS for a few seconds, the same known, separately tracked chase/reversal deformation family every long cavalry pursuit shows, which the demo author cannot stage away. The second-strike moment itself is a 1-2 tick FIGHTING window no sampling grid can reliably catch cross-platform, so the durable second-strike guard is the live GUT regression (test_cycle_charge_flee_battle.gd), not a declared expectation here.
